title: Filling expansion gaps in laminate flooring
subtitle: Fit threshold strips and skirting beads to hide perimeter gaps
excerpt: How to deal with the perimeter expansion gaps left around a laminate floating floor: choosing the right threshold and perimeter strip, fitting cover profiles at doorways and against skirting boards, and handling gaps that have grown wider than expected.

glossaryTerms.definition: A deliberate gap left around the perimeter of a floating floor to allow the boards to expand and contract with temperature and humidity changes. Typically 10 to 12 mm. If the gap is closed, the floor will buckle.

A metal or plastic profile fitted at doorways to cover the expansion gap between a floating floor and an adjacent floor covering or a fixed threshold. Available in single-floor height (flat bar), step-down (reducer), and double-height (T-bar for two floors at the same height) profiles.

content.text: A floating laminate floor is installed with a deliberate
expansion gap
around the perimeter and at doorways. This gap must remain clear of debris and rigid obstructions. Covering it with a
threshold strip
or skirting bead is purely cosmetic; the cover profile must not be fixed rigidly to both the floor and the wall simultaneously.
When to use it
At doorways, where the laminate meets a tiled, carpeted, or timber floor at a different height.
Against skirting boards where the manufacturer's gap is too wide to be hidden by the skirting overlap.
At the junction between a laminate floor and a fixed kitchen unit base plinth.
What you need

The correct threshold profile for the transition: T-bar, reducer, or end cap. Cut to length with a tenon saw. Most kits use a metal base rail fixed to the subfloor with the profile clipping over it. Bring the base rail fixings supplied with the kit.
Step-by-step
Step 1, measure and cut.
Measure the doorway width or the length of the gap to be covered. Cut the profile to length with a tenon saw. Mitre the ends if the profile runs into a door lining at an angle.
Step 2, fix the base rail.

Drill pilot holes through the base rail and into the subfloor. Fix with the screws supplied. The rail must be fixed to the subfloor only, not to the laminate boards. Where the subfloor is concrete, use a masonry screw or a plugged fixing.
Step 3, clip the profile.
Press the decorative profile over the base rail and snap into position. The profile should move slightly along the rail, allowing the laminate below to expand without pushing the profile off.
When the gap is wider than standard

If the expansion gap has grown wider than the threshold or skirting cover profile can span (typically more than 15 to 20 mm), the floor has expanded beyond its designed range. This usually means moisture has entered the room and raised the humidity above the design condition. Identify and address the source of moisture first. The floor may need to be lifted, trimmed at the edges to restore the correct gap, and re-laid once the humidity is controlled.
Common mistakes
Fixing the threshold profile to the laminate.
Screwing through the laminate boards closes the expansion gap and will cause the floor to buckle as it expands. The base rail fixes to the subfloor; the profile rides over the edge of the laminate.
Gluing skirting bead to both the skirting board and the floor.
The skirting bead should cover the gap but be free to move with the floor. Glue or pin it to the skirting board only.
